result.title: PSYCH 109
result.summary:
# 🧠 Integrated Study Guide

This guide first teaches each source in detail, then explicitly maps connection points, visual evidence, debates, and exam use across the source set.

## 1. Source-by-Source Professor Notes

### Source 1: Cognitive Development - Lecture 1 - The Big Picture-5.pptx
Title / course: PSYCH 109  
Author / presenter evidence: Dr Christopher Erb, Senior Lecturer; Director, Cognitive, Manual, and Neural Dynamics (CMND) Lab; christopher.erb@auckland.ac.nz. (Slide evidence: “Dr. Christopher Erb … CMND Lab.”)  
Extracted text length: 10,375 characters. Two embedded images shown on Slide 1 (see Visual section). This card reconstructs the lecture in source-faithful detail so you can revise it without re-opening the PPT.

#### Opening frame / central question
What is developmental (cognitive) psychology’s “big picture”? Specifically: Where does a developmental approach fit within the broader discipline of psychology, what core theoretical questions do developmental scientists ask, how should we “think like” a developmental scientist (i.e., what assumptions and critical habits should we adopt), and what are the practical benefits of studying development across the lifespan?

This lecture sets conceptual foundations for the course: how to situate developmental questions across levels of analysis (neural → cultural), across timelines (individual ontogeny → phylogeny), and across research goals (basic → applied). It asks: how do we avoid taking the mind for granted, and how do we approach the problem of building, explaining, and studying minds that develop?

#### Source structure / lecture flow
Below I follow the slides in order, using the slide headings and visible content as the lecture’s logical flow. Each item identifies the slide title (where visible) and then explains the slide’s teaching points.

1. Slide 1 — Title slide: “Psychology over the life-span: Growing up, growing older, growing wiser. Dr. Christopher Erb : Lecture 1 PSYCH 109”  
   - Function: course framing; visual(s) included (two embedded images) that appear to illustrate cultural/behavioral diversity across environments (see Visual section).

2. Slide 2 — “Dr. Christopher Erb” (about presenter)  
   - Biographical and contact info: rural Ohio origin; undergrad University of Cincinnati; grad Brown University; postdoc University of North Carolina at Greensboro; role at University of Auckland as lecturer and CMND director.  
   - Function: provenance and contact for course logistics.

3. Slide 3 — “Adaptive Minds” (quote/idea)  
   - Main idea: humans’ global distribution and ecological success point to adaptiveness of mind and behavior. Sets tone: development must explain adaptation across diverse environments.

4. Slide 4 — “Flexible Minds” with three small citations:  
   - Inhibit prepotent responses (Schachar & Logan, 1990)  
   - Switch between tasks (Zelazo, Müller, Frye, & Marcovitch, 2003)  
   - Delay gratification (Mischel, Ebbesen, & Raskoff Zeiss, 1972)  
   - Function: introduce concrete cognitive-control abilities that develop and support flexible/adaptive behavior.

5. Slide 5 — “My Research” — lists research themes: Attention and Distraction; Causal Learning and Reasoning; Cognitive Control; Numerical Cognition.  
   - Function: show instructor’s research connects to lecture themes (attention, control, embodied number cognition).

6. Slide 6 — “Outline for Week” — Lectures 1–3: “The Big Picture”; “From Reflexes to Reason” (Piaget & contemporary views); “Core Topics” (Memory, Physics, Number).  
   - Function: roadmap of upcoming content.

7. Slide 7 — Learning Objectives (questions to answer): How does developmental psychology fit into broader psychology? How to avoid taking the mind for granted? What three theoretical issues did we discuss? Benefits of studying developmental psychology?

8. Slide 8 — “What’s examinable?” logistical slide: all lecture material except slides with a red dot; outlines on Canvas; use end-of-lecture questions for self-test.

9. Slides 9–14 — “Psychology: What is the mind and how does it work?” — introduces a taxonomy:
   - Aspects of mind: Perception, Action, Cognition, Emotion — mapped to subfields (perceptual, cognitive, affective psych).
   - Levels of analysis: Cultural, Social, Behavioural, Neural, Physiological, Genetic — presented as a vertical scale from higher to lower.
   - Timeline distinctions: Ontogenetic (individual development from conception to death) vs Phylogenetic / Evolutionary (history of species). Slide explicitly defines developmental psychology as “How does the mind change over the lifespan?” and evolutionary psychology / phylogenetic development as “How was the mind shaped over evolution?”
   - Comparative psychology: asks how different kinds of minds function (human vs non-human).

10. Slide 14 (continued) — “Goal: Basic Research vs Applied Research” — examples of applied fields: educational, clinical, consumer, organizational psychology.

11. Slide 15 — “The Developmental Approach” — analysis (breaking system into components) vs synthesis (how components integrate): perception, action, cognition, emotion are parts to be integrated.

12. Slides 16–18 — “Developmental encourages a holistic, integrative view of the mind” — lists developmental subfields (perceptual, cognitive, moral, social, action, emotional) and overlays the earlier levels-of-analysis taxonomy (cultural → genetic) and applied fields (clinical, educational, comparative, evolutionary). Emphasizes multi-level integration.

13. Slides 19–29 — “How to think like a developmental scientist: Don’t Take the Mind for Granted (Appreciate the Problem)” — long sequence using physics metaphors:
   - Physics metaphor: gravity is ubiquitous; Newton recognized a problem, providing a framework to test theories. Analogously, the mind is ubiquitous and we must recognize the problem of explaining it.
   - “Building a mind from scratch” thought-experiments: language comprehension & production, computer vision, movement & navigation, developmental robotics, developmentally-inspired AI — these examples show how childhood learning presents engineering-style puzzles.
   - Critical thinking example: infant repeatedly drops spoon — is it clumsiness, learning about gravity/acoustics, or social manipulation (learning how others react)? The instructor explicitly encourages multiple hypotheses and critical testing (link to “Tooth Fairy” story/episode).

14. Slides 30–34 — “Theoretical Questions” — three major theoretical binary tensions framed (and partially problematized):
   - Quantitative vs Qualitative Change: quantitative = numerical differences (e.g., tadpole growth); qualitative = emergence of new structures/abilities (e.g., metamorphosis).
   - Nature vs Nurture: Are we products of genes or experience? Slide emphasizes false dichotomy and interplay (gene expression modulated by experience — epigenetics). Example: cilantro taste aversion (4–14% report soapy taste due to olfactory-receptor genes) vs cultural practices (how often people eat insects).
   - Domain Specific vs Domain General: Are minds organized with many specialized “apps” (physics, number, language, causality, social agents) or a few general-purpose learning algorithms?

15. Slide 35 — Quick review summarizing “Don’t take the mind for granted” and the three theoretical questions.

16. Slide 36 — “Why study developmental psychology?” — contrasts mechanistic view (mind = machine; parts with fixed functions) vs complexity view (mind = ecosystem; components interact and cause reverberating changes).

17. Slides 37–38 — “Why study developmental psychology? To understand others (and yourself) better” — emphasizes developmental psychology’s utility in reducing judgment and increasing understanding of variation in cognition, belief, and behavior; prompts self-reflection (“How can you continue to develop here at UoA?”).

18. Slide 39 — Learning objectives repeated.

This is the lecture’s logical path: framing → taxonomy of psychology → developmental approach (multi-level, integrative) → epistemic habit: don’t take the mind for granted → three core theoretical issues → motives for studying development.

#### Key concepts and definitions
Below is a concept table faithful to the source. For each concept I list: definition (from slide text or standard course usage), simple explanation, where it appears in the source (slide number or section), and why it matters.

- Developmental Psychology  
  - Definition: “How does the mind change over the lifespan?” (slide 11)  
  - Simple explanation: study of psychological change from conception through old age.  
  - Where: slide 11 (timeline / ontogenetic).  
  - Why it matters: central focus of course; frames the rest of the material.

- Ontogenetic Development  
  - Definition: origination and development of an organism across its life (conception → death).  
  - Where: slide 11.  
  - Why: distinguishes individual developmental change from evolutionary change.

- Phylogenetic / Evolutionary Psychology  
  - Definition: study of the evolution of mind/behavior across species or populations (slide 12).  
  - Why: situates development in evolutionary timescales and raises different questions (adaptive function vs developmental mechanisms).

- Levels of Analysis (Cultural → Genetic)  
  - Definition: perspective that psychological phenomena can be analyzed at cultural, social, behavioural, neural, physiological, genetic levels (slides 10–11, 16–18).  
  - Why: developmental explanations often require integrating multiple levels.

- Analysis vs Synthesis  
  - Definition: analysis = breaking a system into components; synthesis = integrating components into a functioning whole (slide 15).  
  - Why: developmental science must do both — describe components (e.g., perception) and explain how they combine over time.

- “Don’t Take the Mind for Granted” (Appreciate the Problem)  
  - Definition: an epistemic stance: treat the mind as an explanandum, not an assumed black box (slides 19–29).  
  - Why: promotes critical thinking and experimental approaches; motivates developmental robotics and AI analogies.

- Quantitative Change  
  - Definition: numerical, continuous change (e.g., growth in size) (slide 30).  
  - Why: one way to conceptualize change; important for measurement and statistical modeling.

- Qualitative Change  
  - Definition: emergent change — new structure or process arises (slide 30).  
  - Why: Piagetian-like questions about stages or transformational shifts.

- Nature vs Nurture (Interaction / Epigenetics)  
  - Definition: debate over genetic vs environmental contributions (slide 31–32).  
  - Why: course stresses interplay and epigenetic modulation rather than strict dichotomy.

- Domain-Specific vs Domain-General  
  - Definition: whether cognitive architecture consists of specialized modules or general-purpose learning mechanisms (slides 33–34).  
  - Why: central theoretical divide shaping research design and predictions.

- Cognitive Control / Inhibition / Switching / Delay of Gratification  
  - Definitions: cognitive control = ability to override impulses; inhibition = suppress prepotent responses (Schachar & Logan, 1990); task switching = shifting between rules/tasks (Zelazo et al., 2003); delay gratification = foregoing immediate reward (Mischel et al., 1972).  
  - Where: slide 4.  
  - Why: concrete examples of developing mechanisms that enable flexible behavior.

- Mechanistic view vs Complexity view  
  - Definitions: mechanistic = mind as machine with fixed functional parts; complexity = mind as an ecosystem with interdependent components and feedback loops (slide 36).  
  - Why: shapes research approach (reductionist experiments vs systems-level longitudinal work).

#### Important studies / examples / cases (source mentions these — reconstructed as taught)
The lecture references a few canonical empirical works by name. The slide provides citations but not full protocols; below I present the canonical research question, typical methods, main findings, interpretation, limitations, and how the slide expects you to use them in exam answers. I stick to the form and do not invent further data beyond standard, widely-known descriptions.

1) Schachar & Logan (1990) — inhibition / prepotent responses  
- Question: How do children and adults inhibit prepotent (dominant) responses?  
- Method / process (canonical): studies often use stop-signal or go/no-go paradigms to measure inhibitory control across ages. Schachar & Logan are known for developmental work on inhibition.  
- Result / answer: inhibitory control improves with age; younger children show slower or incomplete inhibition relative to older children/adults.  
- Meaning: inhibition is a core component of cognitive control that develops across childhood and supports flexible/appropriate behavior.  
- Exam use: cite as empirical support that cognitive control abilities are measurable and develop with age; use when discussing domain-general control or development of executive functions.

2) Zelazo, Müller, Frye, & Marcovitch (2003) — switching / cognitive flexibility  
- Question: How do children switch between tasks or rules (executive function / cognitive flexibility)?  
- Method / process (canonical): tasks like the Dimensional Change Card Sort (DCCS) ask children to sort cards by one dimension (e.g., color), then switch to sorting by another (e.g., shape). Zelazo and colleagues investigated developmental changes in switching ability.  
- Result / answer: young children (around 3 years) perseverate (stick to prior rule); by 4–5 years many successfully switch. Development reflects improvements in rule representation and cognitive control.  
- Meaning: cognitive flexibility is a key developmental milestone supporting flexible strategies.  
- Exam use: use to illustrate task-switching as a measure of domain-general executive control that develops in preschool years.

3) Mischel, Ebbesen, & Raskoff Zeiss (1972) — delay of gratification (marshmallow task)  
- Question: Can children delay immediate gratification for a larger later reward?  
- Method / process (canonical): children offered a small immediate reward or a larger reward if they wait (experimenter leaves the room). Researchers measured waiting times and later outcomes.  
- Result / answer: considerable individual differences; some children wait (show self-control) while others take immediate reward; later follow-ups suggest associations between early delay ability and later outcomes (though these associations are moderated by other factors).  
- Meaning: self-control/delay ability is an important developing function for long-term planning and adaptive functioning.  
- Exam use: cite as classic evidence that cognitive control and delay of gratification develop and have consequential correlates; use while discussing executive function, socialization, or domain-general capacities.

Note on limitations: The lecture presents these studies as exemplars; it does not provide full methods or all critiques. When using these in essays, mention standard methodological limits (e.g., ecological validity, cultural variation, longitudinal confounds) if relevant.

4) Spoon-dropping infant example (thought experiment)  
- Question: Why does an infant repeatedly drop a spoon?  
- Process: thought experiment encourages generating multiple hypotheses: motor immaturity (clumsiness) vs active learning about gravity/acoustics vs social manipulation (testing caregiver reactions).  
- Meaning: illustrates critical-scientific stance — observe behavior and generate/test multiple functional hypotheses rather than assume a single cause.  
- Exam use: a handy vignette to demonstrate “don’t take the mind for granted” and to practice hypothesis generation and experimental design.

5) Cilantro taste example (slide 32)  
- Question: Why do some people say cilantro tastes like soap?  
- Meaning explained on slide: 4–14% experience soapy taste due to olfactory receptor genes (biological contribution) while eating insects is heavily cultural (environmental contribution).  
- Exam use: use as a concise, memorable illustration that nature and nurture interact; good example for short-answer prompts.

#### Visual / diagram / table explanation from this source
Two embedded images are included on Slide 1; they likely form part of the lecture’s opening visual. I describe each graphic as presented, explain the pedagogical purpose, and tell you how to read them in exam answers.

Visual 1 — Colorful dancer in regalia (embedded image on PPT slide 1)
- What is visually shown: a child (or young person) in bright, patterned, fringed regalia mid-motion (photograph). The image emphasizes movement, cultural dress, sensory richness (color, texture).  
- What argument/concept it supports: illustrates human cultural diversity, embodied action, and the interaction of perception, action, and cultural context in development. It visually reinforces the “adaptive minds” theme — humans adapt culturally and behaviorally to varied environments.  
- How a student should read it step by step:  
  1. Note the person is engaged in a culturally-specific activity (dancing) — link to social and cultural levels of analysis.  
  2. Observe sensorimotor demands (movement, coordination) — link to action/perceptual development.  
  3. Consider how cultural practices shape learning and cognitive development (domain-general vs domain-specific debate).  
- How it could connect to other sources: ties to comparative/cultural developmental studies (e.g., how cultural practices scaffold early motor and social development), to lectures on social and moral development, and to examples of embodied cognition in numerical representation.

Visual 2 — Three children by a river with green hills (embedded image on PPT slide 1)
- What is visually shown: three children standing by a riverbank in a rural environment with lush hills in background. Clothing and setting suggest a non-Western, possibly low-resource ecological context.  
- What argument/concept it supports: emphasizes ecological and cultural variation in developmental contexts; links back to “adaptation” and the need to study development across diverse environments and levels of analysis (cultural, social, behavioural).  
- How to read it step by step:  
  1. Identify environmental affordances (river, rural terrain) that shape learning (navigation, water safety, social routines).  
  2. Consider social/cultural practices likely to differ from urban/Western contexts, influencing nutrition, play, schooling — hence diverse developmental trajectories.  
  3. Use the image as a prompt to discuss how general theories (e.g., Piagetian stages) must account for ecological variability.  
- How it connects to other sources: connects to cross-cultural developmental literature, applied developmental psychology (education, public health), and evolutionary perspectives on adaptation to local environments.

Note on image safety: these are illustrative images only; do not identify the pictured individuals.

Overall visual purpose: the two images together are a rhetorical device. They visually underline the opening claim: “From the cold Arctic to the sweltering Sahara … humans are everywhere” (Slide 3). They push students to think beyond WEIRD samples and to integrate cultural/ecological diversity into developmental theorizing.

#### What this source uniquely contributes
- Framing and epistemic stance: This lecture provides the course’s foundational frame — it is less about details of Piaget or a particular experiment and more about how to approach developmental questions. It explicitly teaches the habit “don’t take the mind for granted,” using physics metaphors and engineering analogies (building minds, developmental robotics). That orientation is unique: it gives students a scientific attitude and heuristic for generating hypotheses and designing studies.
- Multi-level taxonomy: The slides give a compact but wide-ranging map of levels of analysis (cultural → genetic), timelines (ontogeny → phylogeny), and research goals (basic vs applied), which is a practical scaffold you should use when constructing exam answers or study designs.
- Theoretical triad: It identifies three canonical theoretical issues (Quantitative vs Qualitative; Nature vs Nurture; Domain-specific vs Domain-general) as the core debates to frame later material. This triad becomes the organizing lens for later lectures (Piaget, core domains, memory, number).
- Pedagogical examples: the spoon-dropping thought experiment, cilantro example, and references to canonical inhibition/switching/delay studies provide accessible exemplars to apply the framework.

#### Connections to other sources in the pack (predicted)
Given the lecture outline and content, this source will likely:
- Support Lecture 2 (Piaget): The quantitative vs qualitative debate and stage-related thinking connect directly to Piagetian theory — this lecture sets up the question that Lecture 2 will address. (supports / frames)
- Provide an attitude for Lecture 3 (Memory, Physics, Number): The “don’t take the mind for granted” heuristic and domain-specific vs domain-general framing will be used to evaluate domain-specific core knowledge accounts (e.g., numerical cognition) versus general learning mechanisms. (provides mechanism for comparing)
- Connect to empirical methods: the cognitive-control examples (Schachar, Zelazo, Mischel) will be expanded with experimental tasks in later lectures. (extends / provides examples)
- Contrast with evolutionary/comparative sources: slides on phylogenetic and comparative psychology anticipate later readings on innate constraints or cross-species comparisons. (contrasts / contextualizes)

#### Common misunderstandings (source-specific) and corrections
- Misunderstanding: “Nature vs Nurture” is a 50/50 partition you can quantify easily.  
  - Correction: The lecture stresses this is a false dichotomy. Genes and environment interact throughout development (e.g., epigenetics). Always discuss interaction and mechanisms of interplay, not simple percentages.
- Misunderstanding: Qualitative change always implies stage theories like Piaget.  
  - Correction: Qualitative change can be described in multiple theoretical frameworks (emergentist models, neural network reorganizations) and does not require fixed stages unless evidence supports discrete transitions.
- Misunderstanding: Domain-specific = fixed modules from birth; domain-general = equivalent to “no constraints.”  
  - Correction: Domain-specific theories can allow for development and learning; domain-general models can include biases. The debate is about relative emphasis, not an all-or-nothing claim.
- Misunderstanding: The “don’t take the mind for granted” slogan means everything is learnable from scratch.  
  - Correction: It means question commonly-held assumptions and probe mechanisms; it does not pre-commit you to nativist or empiricist positions.

#### Exam / revision use
- Revision priorities: memorize the three theoretical issues (quantitative vs qualitative; nature vs nurture; domain-specific vs domain-general) and be able to define and give an example of each. Understand levels of analysis (cultural → genetic) and timelines (ontogeny vs phylogeny). Be able to explain the “don’t take the mind for granted” heuristic and use the spoon example to generate hypotheses and study designs. Know the three exemplar tasks/studies (inhibition, task-switching, delay of gratification) and what they illustrate about cognitive control development.
- Likely question types and how to answer them:
  1. Short answer: “Explain ontogenetic vs phylogenetic development.” — Give definitions, contrast timelines, and give an example (ontogenetic: infant motor milestones; phylogenetic: evolution of language capacity traits).
  2. Essay: “Discuss whether cognitive development is domain-specific or domain-general.” — Use the slides’ framing: define both positions, give empirical examples (number/physics as candidate domains; executive function tasks as candidate domain-general mechanisms), discuss interaction and conclude with hybrid/interactionist position. Reference the Cilantro example to show gene × culture interplay.
  3. Applied design question: “A child repeatedly drops a spoon. Propose hypotheses and an experiment to test them.” — Use the spoon vignette: list at least three hypotheses (motor learning, experiment in acoustics / test understanding of gravity, social manipulation), propose operationalized tests (e.g., measure motor control, test causal inference about gravity via object-drop manipulations, observe caregiver responses), and note predicted outcomes & confounds. This answer should reflect the “don’t take the mind for granted” stance.
- High-scoring answers should: (a) use the lecture’s multi-level language (cultural → genetic) where relevant, (b) state the theoretical binaries and take a justified position that recognizes interplay (no strict dichotomies), (c) cite classic empirical examples (Mischel, Zelazo, Schachar) to ground arguments, and (d) discuss methodological or ecological limitations.

---

If you want, I can convert this card into a printable one-page revision sheet, make a one-hour study plan based on these priorities, or generate practice exam questions with model answers using only material from this lecture.

### Source 2: Russell Lecture 12 - Human Nature.pdf
Source identity (explicit evidence)
- Display name (file): Russell Lecture 12 - Human Nature.pdf  
- Slide author / lecturer (visible): Russell Gray — Lecture 12, Psychology 109 (title slide)  
- Topic label visible on slides: “Human Nature — are we inherently violent and selfish? Or peaceful and co‑operative?” (PDF page 1)  
- Extracted / cited works visible in slides: Lorenz (1966, On Aggression), Samuel Bowles & Herb Gintis (Science, 2009), Steven Pinker (2012, The Better Angels of Our Nature), Keith Jensen et al. (Science 2007), Henrich et al. (2010), Bandura, Surbeck (chimp/bonobo comparison).  
- I am reconstructing the lecture slides and the explicit text/images they contain. I do not infer another source.

#### Opening frame / central question
The lecture frames a single, precise question and four sub-questions: Are humans inherently violent and selfish, or peaceful and cooperative? The lecture then asks:
- What evidence supports a view of humans as naturally violent/selfish? (the “case for”)
- What evidence opposes that view? (the “case against”)
- How do comparative primate data (chimpanzee vs bonobo) and evolutionary models inform the question?
- Behavioural economics tests (dictator and ultimatum games) and cross‑cultural data: are people selfish or cooperative in practice?

The lecture explicitly places classic texts (Lorenz 1966, Freud) and modern empirical work (Bowles & Gintis; Jensen et al.; Pinker) in tension to answer that overarching question.

#### Source structure / lecture flow (slide-by-slide reconstruction)
1. Title slide (page 1): Human Nature — "are we inherently violent and selfish? Or peaceful and co‑operative?" — image montage of angry faces (sets the theme: aggression vs cooperation).
2. Outline slide (page 2): Four items — (1) Two takes on “Lord of the Flies”; (2) The case for violent, selfish human nature; (3) The case against violent, selfish human nature; (4) Are we selfish or cooperative?
3. Key terms and concepts (page 3): lists items that structure the lecture:
   - Aggression — is it one thing with one cause?
   - Hydraulic model of aggression; catharsis
   - Changes in violence over history (Pinker’s claim)
   - Differences between common chimpanzee and bonobo
   - Ancestral state reconstruction (parsimony inference)
   - Dictator game; Ultimatum game
4. Short multimedia / real‑world case references (pages 5–6): links to videos and a Guardian article about the real castaways (Tongan teenagers) popularly invoked as a counterpoint to Lord of the Flies.
5. The case for (pages 7–13+): classic psychological and ethological arguments:
   - Freud: "primitive, savage impulses" — id vs superego tension.
   - Konrad Lorenz (1966) — psycho‑hydraulic model and the famous quotation from On Aggression describing an inherited aggression drive that intelligence may fail to control.
   - Bowles & Gintis-style evolutionary game theory: if intergroup mortality from warfare is sufficiently high, selection can favor "warlike altruists" (groups with more altruists outcompete others). Bowles' Science paper is shown (figures & table summarizing fraction of mortality due to warfare).
   - Wrangham (mentioned as a “pacifist scientist who believes humans evolved to be violent” — slide pointing to controversy).
6. Comparative primatology (pages 12–18): bonobo vs common chimpanzee differences; citation to Parish, de Waal, Haig (2000) and Surbeck (comparison). Slide shows great ape phylogeny and an ancestral‑state reconstruction caveat: parsimony inference is uncertain (we cannot confidently reconstruct ancestral levels of aggression).
7. The case against (pages 19–24): conceptual critiques and alternatives:
   - What is aggression? many senses/uses of the word (examples of different meanings).
   - Criticisms of the hydraulic model: aggression is not a single thing; catharsis hypothesis unsupported; hydraulic model is poor evolutionary design (no feedback, not strategic).
   - Bandura’s observational learning / modelling as alternate mechanism for learning aggressive behaviour (slides include Bandura reference).
   - Steven Pinker (2012) cited that violence has declined historically.
8. Behavioural economics & experimental evidence (pages 25–31):
   - Dictator game and Ultimatum game introduced; Henrich et al. (2010) shown to demonstrate cross‑cultural variation in offers in dictator game (people are not uniformly selfish).
   - Keith Jensen et al. (Science 2007) figure (Fig. 3) comparing rejection rates across games for chimpanzees (black bars) and humans (white bars) — empirical test whether non‑human primates show human‑like fairness.
9. Synthesis and takeaways (pages 32–33): How cooperation can evolve (reputation, partner choice, punishment, repeated interactions) and the concluding question: "So what is human nature?"

#### Key concepts and definitions (concept table)
- Aggression  
  - Definition (as used in slides): a class of behaviours/states variously described (physical attack, hostile intent, competitive behaviours, policy rhetoric).  
  - Where it appears: slide titled “What is ‘aggression’?” and across the lecture.  
  - Why it matters: the argument for 'innate violence' depends on how aggression is conceptualised — conflating different senses produces confusion.

- Hydraulic model of aggression (Lorenz)  
  - Definition: aggression builds up like hydraulic pressure and must be discharged; innate drive explanation.  
  - Where: Lorenz slides, Lorenz 1966 quotation.  
  - Why it matters: historical ethological claim linking instinctual buildup to overt aggression — attacked by modern critics for lack of mechanism and poor evolutionary logic.

- Catharsis  
  - Definition: hypothesis that performing aggression reduces subsequent aggression (discharge).  
  - Where: listed in key terms and criticisms.  
  - Why: often used to justify controlled aggression, but evidence is weak; used to critique hydraulic model.

- Ancestral state reconstruction (parsimony inference)  
  - Definition: phylogenetic method inferring ancestral traits by minimizing changes.  
  - Where: slides on primate comparisons (page 18).  
  - Why: used to argue about whether our last common ancestor was warlike/peaceful — but parsimony often uncertain.

- Warlike altruists / c* (Bowles & Gintis)  
  - Definition: theoretical maximum cost to individuals that altruistic behaviour could bear and still spread via between‑group selection (c* is the derived measure).  
  - Where: Bowles slide quoting Science paper and table summarizing warfare mortality.  
  - Why: gives a formal evolutionary model linking intergroup conflict frequency/severity to evolution of group‑beneficial costly behaviour (including violent defence).

- Dictator game / Ultimatum game  
  - Definitions: Dictator — one player unilaterally splits a pot; Ultimatum — second player can accept or reject (if reject both get nothing).  
  - Where: dedicated slides and figure from Jensen et al., and Henrich et al. cross‑cultural data.  
  - Why: standard experimental probes for fairness, altruism, and punishment.

- Observational learning / Modelling (Bandura)  
  - Definition: learning behaviours via observing others, not just innate drives.  
  - Where: slide listing Bandura as alternative explanation.  
  - Why: offers social/cultural mechanism for aggression transmission; counters purely instinctive accounts.

#### Important studies / examples (each reconstructed as teaching units)

1. Real castaway case (Tongan teenagers)
- Question: Does real‑world human behaviour under castaway conditions mirror Lord of the Flies (collapse into violence) or show co‑operation?
- Method/process: News reports and documentaries on six Tongan boys who survived as castaways for 15 months (links on slides to Guardian, YouTube).
- Result: The boys cooperated and survived without descending into the Hobbesian nightmare — commonly used as evidence against inherent human savagery.
- Meaning: Social context and cultural norms can produce cooperative outcomes in extreme settings; caution against simplistic “innate violent” narratives.
- Exam use: Use as vivid empirical counterexample to Lord of the Flies anecdotes; good to pair with theory (hydraulic vs social learning).

2. Lorenz (1966) — On Aggression
- Question: Is aggression an inherited drive that builds up?
- Method/process: Ethological observations and theoretical argument (psycho‑hydraulic metaphor).
- Result: Lorenz advocated an innate aggression drive (quote shown on slide).
- Meaning: Historically influential but vulnerable to critique — lacks strategic feedback mechanisms and empirical support for catharsis.
- Exam use: Present Lorenz as a foundational position; then critically evaluate using modern evidence.

3. Bowles & Gintis / Bowles (Science 2009 text used on slides)
- Question: Can between‑group conflict select for altruistic (including warlike) behaviour?
- Method/process: Formal models of multi‑level selection combined with empirical re‑estimates of prehistoric/ethnographic warfare mortality (table shown).
- Result: If warfare mortality is sufficiently high, selection can favour “warlike altruists” (Bowles derives c*, the maximum cost consistent with proliferation).
- Meaning: Provides an evolutionary mechanism linking intergroup conflict to cooperative/altruistic traits; suggests possible genetic contribution to group‑level aggression.
- Limitations: Models depend on assumptions about warfare frequency/intensity and ignore cultural transmission complexities.
- Exam use: Use Bowles to show rigorous formal evolutionary argument that challenges purely cultural explanations; discuss assumptions and empirical uncertainty.

4. Chimpanzee vs Bonobo comparisons (Surbeck; Parish, de Waal, Haig)
- Question: Do our closest living relatives suggest humans evolved toward violence or peace?
- Method/process: Comparative observations: common chimpanzees show male‑biased intergroup aggression; bonobos are more tolerant/sexually mediated tension reduction.
- Result: Divergent social systems exist in Pan genus; ancestral reconstruction is uncertain by parsimony.
- Meaning: Comparative data complicate simple evolutionary claims; we cannot definitively infer ancestral human disposition from either species.
- Exam use: Use to illustrate limits of comparative method and importance of phylogenetic uncertainty.

5. Jensen et al. (Science 2007) — chimpanzee vs human ultimatum/dictator game comparison (Fig. 3 shown)
- Question: Do chimpanzees show human‑like sensitivity to fairness in economic games?
- Method/process: Variants of sharing/offer games (5/5, 2/8, 8/2, 10/0) with subjects’ rejection rates recorded; slide reproduces Fig. 3 where black bars = chimps, white bars = humans.
- Result: Humans (white bars) generally show higher rejection rates for unfair offers than chimpanzees (black bars) in several game conditions.
- Meaning: Evidence that humans have stronger behavioural responses to fairness than captive chimpanzees in these paradigms; but experimental setup, species differences in payoff valuation, and interpretation of 'rejection' must be considered.
- Limitations: Ecological validity, small samples, motivational differences between species.
- Exam use: Use the figure to argue humans evolved specialised fairness‑sensitive behaviours beyond what is seen in our closest relatives — but always discuss methodological caveats.

6. Henrich et al. (2010) — cross‑cultural dictator game
- Question: Are humans everywhere selfish in dictator game?
- Method: Cross‑society dictator game measures across many populations (figure on slides).
- Result: Behaviour varies across cultures; many populations give nonzero amounts (not purely selfish).
- Meaning: Challenges simple universal selfishness claims; emphasises cultural variation and learned norms.
- Exam use: Use to argue for cultural modulation and to problematise purely genetic accounts.

#### Common misunderstandings (source‑specific)
- Mistake: Treating “aggression” as a single trait. Correction: the lecture emphasises multiple meanings (affective anger, instrumental violence, rhetorical aggression), and conflating them leads to confused arguments.
- Mistake: Taking Lorenz’s hydraulic model as current evidence for genetic determinism. Correction: the lecture cites strong conceptual and empirical criticisms — hydraulic model is historically important but poor as an up‑to‑date mechanism.
- Mistake: Assuming chimpanzee behaviour straightforwardly indicates ancestral human behaviour. Correction: bonobo/chimp differences and uncertain ancestral reconstruction mean comparative evidence is ambiguous.
- Mistake: Interpreting Bowles as proving humans are genetically “warlike.” Correction: Bowles presents a conditional model (depends on warfare intensity/frequency), not a definitive proof; cultural transmission and model assumptions matter.

#### Opening frame / central question
- Central question the lecture addresses: To what extent are human psychological traits (especially intelligence and aggression) determined by genetic differences versus environmental differences — i.e. how should psychologists think about "nature and nurture"?
- Sub-questions raised repeatedly in the lecture:
  - How do genetic concepts (gene, genotype, allele, dominant/recessive, polygenic) map onto psychological traits?
  - Which research designs can separate genetic from environmental influences (adoption studies, twin studies, separated identical twins)?
  - What does heritability mean — and what does it not mean?
  - What do specific cases (PKU, MAOA, IQ studies, Flynn effect) teach about gene × environment interactions and the social implications of hereditarian claims?

#### Source structure / lecture flow (slide-by-slide reconstruction)
The lecture follows a clear plan repeated on slides; I preserve slide titles and the flow the slides imply:

1. Title slide

2. Opening question and significance

3. Learning objectives (explicit slide text)

   - Understand methodological problems with twin studies.

4. Lecture plan (iterative slide repeated)

   - 2. PKU — what it tells us about genes/environment
   - 3. Genes and aggression (MAOA)
   - 4. The heritability hang-up

5. Basic genetic terminology (several slides)
   - Definitions shown on slides: genotype, phenotype, chromosome, gene, allele, locus, homozygous, heterozygous, dominant, recessive, polygenic inheritance.
   - Example used: can you roll your tongue? (dominant allele example: RR and Rr can roll; rr cannot).

6. PKU case (Phenylketonuria)
   - Clinical description on slides: "Inborn metabolic disorder; single recessive gene on chromosome 12; incidence 1/10,000 white infants in USA; vomiting, seizures, hyperactivity, retardation."
   - Biochemistry on slide: phenylalanine → (PAH enzyme = phenylalanine hydroxylase) → tyrosine; defective enzyme → build-up of phenylpyruvate toxic to developing nervous system.
   - Herzberg & Diamond (1993) example: early dietary intervention reduces but does not eliminate cognitive deficits; practical difficulties of adhering to extreme diet.

7. Genes and aggression (MAOA example)
   - Slide references: "Violent 'warrior gene'? Science, 2002. Monoamine oxidase A (MAOA) metabolises neurotransmitters (norepinephrine, serotonin, dopamine). Location: Xp11.23-11.4."
   - Dunedin Study context: Richie Poulton's long-term cohort (1037 babies born 1972–1973) used to investigate gene × environment (maltreatment) interactions.
   - Dunedin maltreatment stats shown: ages 3–11: 8% severe maltreatment, 28% probable maltreatment, 64% no maltreatment.
   - Implicit lesson: MAOA × maltreatment interaction research is controversial and is a clear example of the need to include environmental measures when claiming genetic effects on behaviour.

8. Correlation slide
   - Making the methodological point: correlations do not necessarily indicate causal relationships.
   - Visual examples: weight × height correlation ~0.7; IQ × height correlation ~0.0 (slide shows these to contrast strong vs null correlations).

9. Heritability slide (explicit definition and formula)
   - Text: "Heritability: The proportion of variation in a population that is attributable to genetic differences. A proportional score e.g. H^2 = G/V = 0.7."
   - Follow-up slide listing important caveats:
     - Heritability estimates do NOT indicate degree to which a trait can be modified (malleability).
     - They do NOT apply to individuals (population statistic).
     - They DO depend on the range of environments and genotypes sampled.
     - They assume additivity (that genotype and environment "add up" in the same way across situations).

10. Heritability diagram (visual)
    - A figure showing shading across axes labelled (Environment: low → high) and (Character value/Genotype: low → high), with vertical arrows labelled G and E and V (total variance). Purpose: to illustrate how variance splits into genetic and environmental components in a population sample and how H^2 is computed as G/V.

11. Heritability of IQ slide
    - "Can we separate the effects of shared genes from shared environments? Three possible ways… 1. Adoption studies 2. Identical (monozygotic) vs non-identical (dizygotic) twins 3. Separated identical twins."

12. Twin/separated twin evidence (slides with concrete numbers)
    - Shields (1962): 40 pairs of separated identical twins r = 0.77; Kamin noted 27/40 raised by close relatives had r = 0.83; remaining 13 pairs r = 0.51 (this caveat shows rearing similarity matters).
    - >100 separated identical twins r ≈ 0.70 (Bouchard et al., 1990).

14. Social/political context slides re IQ and group differences

    - Jim Flynn & the "Flynn effect": Flynn (1984, 1987) shows national gains in IQ: USA 1932–1972 = +11.2 IQ points; NZ 1936–1980 = +11.1.
    - Lewontin's argument: different causes for within-group versus between-group differences.

#### Key concepts and definitions (concept table — definition, short explanation, where it appears in this source, why it matters)
- Gene
  - Definition: stretch of DNA that produces a specific protein (slide text).
  - Source location: basic terminology slides.
  - Why it matters: foundational unit invoked when explaining inherited traits and molecular mechanisms (e.g., PKU, MAOA).
- Genotype
  - Definition: the genetic makeup; what is inherited (set of DNA molecules in nucleus; genotype particular to a trait).
  - Where: terminology slides.
  - Why: central to distinguishing inherited potential from expressed phenotype.
- Phenotype
  - Definition: the organism’s observable traits (physiology, anatomy, behaviour).
  - Where: terminology slides.
  - Why: phenotype is what researchers measure (e.g., IQ scores, aggressive behavior).
- Allele / Locus
  - Definition: allele = one form of a gene; locus = position of a gene on a chromosome.
  - Where: basic genetic terms.
  - Why: explains variation in a population and dominant/recessive inheritance.
- Homozygous / Heterozygous
  - Definitions: homozygous = two identical alleles; heterozygous = two different alleles.
  - Where: terminology slides with examples (e.g., tongue rolling).
  - Why: determines whether recessive/dominant alleles express.
- Dominant / Recessive
  - Definition: dominant allele manifests in heterozygote and homozygote; recessive manifests only in homozygote.
  - Where: slides.
  - Why: used to understand Mendelian examples (contrast with polygenic traits).
- Polygenic inheritance
  - Definition: trait whose phenotype is influenced by many genes (slide: most psychological traits).
  - Why: crucial to appreciate IQ, personality, etc., are not single-gene traits.
- PKU (Phenylketonuria)
  - Definition/case: inherited (single recessive) metabolic disorder; PAH enzyme defect; phenylalanine accumulation produces phenylpyruvate toxic to nervous system.
  - Where: PKU slides and Herzberg & Diamond 1993.
  - Why: classic demonstration of gene × environment — a genetic disorder whose effects can be reduced (but not always eliminated) by environmental (dietary) intervention.
- MAOA (monoamine oxidase A)
  - Definition: enzyme metabolising norepinephrine, serotonin, dopamine; gene on X chromosome (Xp11.23-11.4).
  - Where: "warrior gene" slide, Science 2002 reference, Dunedin study context.
  - Why: widely discussed gene × environment example for aggression; highlights complexity and controversies in behavioural genetics.
- Heritability (H^2)
  - Definition: proportion of variance in a population attributable to genetic differences; H^2 = G / V.
  - Where: Heritability slide, diagram.
  - Why: central metric used in twin/adoption studies and in public debates about "how heritable" traits are.
- SNP (single nucleotide polymorphism)
  - Definition: single base-pair variations in the genome used in association studies.
  - Where: SNP slide.
  - Why: modern approach to locate small-effect variants linked to complex traits.
- GCTA (Genome-Wide Complex Trait Analysis)
  - Definition: method using genome-wide SNP data to estimate the proportion of variance explained by all genotyped SNPs together.
  - Where: GCTA slide and "No 'genius gene' — polygenic" conclusion.
  - Why: shows that many genes of small effect contribute to traits like IQ.
- IQ
  - Definition/measurement: psychometric estimate of cognitive ability; reference to Alfred Binet (1905).
  - Where: IQ slides and multiple subsequent slides.
  - Why: focal trait for debates about heritability, group differences, Flynn effect.

#### Important studies / examples / how the lecture treats them
For each case, I give the short study breakdown following lecture content.

1) PKU (Phenylketonuria)
- Question:
  - What does PKU show about gene × environment interactions for cognitive outcomes?
- Method / process:
  - Clinical-genetic description (single recessive mutation in PAH gene on chromosome 12); biochemical pathway (phenylalanine → phenylpyruvate buildup toxic to nervous system); treatment via early diet was studied historically.
- Result / answer (as slide reports):
  - Early application of a restricted diet reduces cognitive deficits but does not always eliminate them (Herzberg & Diamond, 1993). Diet is extremely restrictive and hard to follow.
- Meaning:
  - PKU is a model example that some genetic effects can be altered by environmental interventions (diet), disproving simplistic "genes = destiny" claims. But biological causation plus environmental modification is complex and imperfect.
- Exam use:
  - Use PKU as a concrete illustration to answer "Explain gene × environment interaction" or "Evaluate claims that genes determine intelligence." Good exam quote: single-gene disorder but environmental modification possible — demonstrates limits of heritability = immutability reasoning.

2) MAOA and aggression (Dunedin Study and Science 2002)
- Question:
  - Does low-activity MAOA allele predict violent or antisocial behaviour, and does childhood maltreatment moderate that effect?
- Method / process:
  - Genetic association studies (Science, 2002 cited), and cohort evidence using Dunedin longitudinal study (N ≈ 1037, Richie Poulton).
  - Dunedin slide gives distribution of maltreatment across childhood: 8% severe, 28% probable, 64% none.
- Result / answer (as presented):
  - The lecture presents MAOA as a controversial example often labelled the "warrior gene" — evidence suggests a gene × environment interaction, but findings are complex and debated.
- Meaning:
  - Demonstrates that behavioural genetics must measure environmental exposures (maltreatment), rely on cohort designs, and be cautious about simplistic media labels.
- Exam use:
  - Use MAOA to discuss gene × environment interaction, the role of longitudinal cohort studies (Dunedin), and social/political misinterpretation of genetic findings.

3) Twin, adoption, and separated twin studies (heritability of IQ)
- Question:
  - Can we separate the effects of shared genes from shared environments and estimate heritability of IQ?
- Method / process:
  - Three designs: adoption studies (compare adoptees with biological vs adoptive parents), twin comparisons (monozygotic vs dizygotic), and separated-identical twins (raised apart).
  - Key slide numbers/claims: Shields (1962) 40 pairs of separated identical twins r = 0.77; Kamin reanalysis: 27/40 raised by close relatives r = 0.83 vs 13 pairs r = 0.51. Bouchard et al. (1990) >100 separated identical twins r ≈ 0.70. Devlin et al. (1997) prenatal contribution ~0.20.
- Result / answer:
  - Twin/separated twin correlations often high for IQ, suggesting substantial genetic contribution to IQ variance; but rearing similarity and selection effects complicate interpretation.
- Meaning:
  - Twin/adoption designs provide important but imperfect evidence. High separated twin correlations are persuasive but must be interpreted in light of possible similarities in rearing and prenatal factors.
- Limitations (explicit in lecture):
  - Methodological problems with twin studies (assumption of equal environments for MZ and DZ twins; selective placement in adoption; non-independence of separated twin rearing).
- Exam use:
  - Good question material: "Evaluate methods used to estimate heritability" — include strengths (natural experiment logic) and limitations (equal environments assumption, selective placement, prenatal effects).

4) Bouchard et al. (1990) / Minnesota twin studies (as slide references)
- Question:
  - What is the magnitude of IQ similarity in separated identical twins across many pairs?
- Method:
  - Aggregated studies of separated MZ twins — large N >100 (slide says >100 separated identical twins 0.70).
- Result:
  - Correlations around r = 0.70 for IQ.
- Meaning:
  - Strong evidence for genetic influence on IQ variance, but see earlier caveats (rearing similarity, publication bias, selective placement).
- Exam use:
  - Use as a numerical anchor when asked to discuss empirical evidence for the heritability of IQ.

5) Flynn effect (James R. Flynn studies)
- Question:
  - Are national IQs stable, and what does change over time tell us about genetic vs environmental causes?
- Method / process:
  - Cross-cohort comparisons of IQ test scores over decades.
- Result (slide numbers):
  - USA 1932–1972: +11.2 IQ points; New Zealand 1936–1980: +11.1 points.
- Meaning:
  - Large generational gains in IQ point strongly to environmental influences (education, nutrition, test familiarity, public health). This undermines simple hereditarian claims that population differences are genetic.
- Exam use:
  - Use Flynn effect to argue that high heritability within a time/sample does not preclude strong environmental effects across time or between groups.

6) Molecular genetics (SNPs, GCTA)
- Question:
  - Do genome-wide studies identify a single highly predictive gene for intelligence?
- Method:
  - SNP-based association studies and genome-wide complex trait analysis (GCTA).
- Result:
  - No single "genius gene" found; intelligence is polygenic with many small-effect variants.
- Meaning:
  - Reinforces the polygenic model and cautions against simplistic genetic determinism.
- Exam use:
  - When asked about modern approaches, explain how GCTA estimates aggregate SNP variance and what it implies about the architecture of complex traits.

7) Herrnstein & Murray (summarised slide)
- Lecture reproduces their argument structure:
  - Premises: 1) Major differences between ethnic groups and classes in IQ; 2) IQ tests measure intellectual ability; 3) IQ highly heritable; 4) Highly heritable traits cannot be changed; therefore ethnic and class differences are genetic and cannot (and should not) be changed.
- Lecturer asks: "Where would you disagree and why?" — invites critique using Lewontin, Flynn effect, heritability caveats, and twin/adoption method limitations.
- Exam use:
  - Typical essay prompt: "Critically evaluate Herrnstein & Murray's argument that group IQ differences are genetic" — use the lecture's evidence to refute each premise.

#### Common misunderstandings (source-specific) and corrections
- Misunderstanding: "Heritability (H^2) tells you what proportion of an individual's trait is genetic."
  - Correction: H^2 is a population statistic describing variance in a sample; it does not partition any individual's trait into "X% genetic, Y% environmental."
- Misunderstanding: "High heritability means the trait cannot be changed."
  - Correction: Heritability does not equal immutability — the Flynn effect shows large population IQ gains despite high heritability estimates within populations.
- Misunderstanding: "Separated identical twin concordance proves pure genetic causation."
  - Correction: Separated twin studies are informative but vulnerable to selective placement and hidden similarities in rearing; Kamin’s reanalysis and Bouchard’s work show variation across pairs.
- Misunderstanding: "If a gene (e.g., MAOA) is associated with aggression, the gene 'causes' violent behaviour."
  - Correction: Association studies show statistical relationships often moderated by environment (maltreatment), and single genes typically exert small probabilistic effects; causal claims must be supported with robust longitudinal, mechanistic, and replication evidence.
- Misunderstanding: "If group A scores lower than group B on IQ and IQ is heritable, the group difference must be genetic."
  - Correction: Lewontin’s point: causes of within-group variance need not explain between-group differences; environmental, cultural, socioeconomic, and measurement factors can explain group gaps.

#### Exam / revision use — priorities and high-scoring answer guidance
Revision priorities (what to memorise and rehearse)
- Learn precise definitions: genotype, phenotype, allele, dominant/recessive, polygenic, SNP, heritability (H^2) and its formula (H^2 = G/V).
- Memorise the PKU example and its lesson (single-gene disease mitigated by early diet; citation: Herzberg & Diamond 1993).
- Be able to summarise MAOA / Dunedin examples and why they require gene × environment framing (include Dunedin maltreatment percentages: 8% severe, 28% probable, 64% none).
- Know the three classic designs for estimating heritability of IQ: adoption, MZ vs DZ twins, separated identical twins — and the strengths/weaknesses of each.
- Remember numerical anchors cited on slides: Shields (1962) separated twins r = 0.77; Bouchard et al. >100 separated twins r ≈ 0.70; Flynn effect ~ +11 IQ points over decades in USA/NZ (Flynn 1984/1987).
- Be able to explain GCTA and why modern genetics points to polygenicity (no single "genius gene").

Likely question types and what a high-scoring answer should include
1) "Define heritability and discuss how it is estimated. What are its limitations?"
   - Definition (H^2 = G/V), explanation of designs (twin/adoption/separated twins; molecular methods like SNP/GCTA), numerical evidence from separated-twin/twin studies, diagram explanation (variance components), and limitations (population-level, depends on sampled genotypes/environments, assumes additivity, equal environments assumption).
2) "Using PKU and one other example, discuss how genes and environment interact to produce behaviour."
   - PKU as single-gene disorder with dietary intervention; MAOA as gene interacting with childhood maltreatment; mention cohort evidence (Dunedin), caution on interpretation; emphasize gene × environment interaction, not simple determinism.
3) "Critically evaluate the claim that ethnic IQ differences are genetic (use Herrnstein & Murray as target)."
   - Lay out Herrnstein & Murray premises; critique each using lecture material: measurement issues (IQ tests measure facets but not all "intelligence"), heritability caveats (within vs between groups; Lewontin), Flynn effect (environmental gains), adoption/twin evidence when children raised together, methodological confounders (selective placement, prenatal effects), and moral/policy implications.
4) "Explain the Flynn effect and its implications for heritability and policy."
   - Present Flynn evidence (+11 IQ points in some nations across mid-20th century), interpret as environmental changes (education, nutrition, test sophistication, complexity of modern life), and use to argue that high heritability does not imply trait immutability or policy irrelevance.

What a high-scoring answer should include (checklist)
- Accurate definitions and formulas (e.g., H^2 = G/V).
- At least one concrete empirical example with numbers (e.g., separated twin r ≈ 0.7; Flynn +11 IQ points).
- Clear methodological critique (equal environment assumption, sample-dependence of heritability, genotype range).
- Distinction between individual vs population statements.
- Discussion of social/political implications and ethical caution (media oversimplification).
- If relevant: mention of molecular genetic evidence (SNPs, GCTA) supporting polygenic models.

#### Final revision tasks (actionable)
- Flashcard set: definitions (gene, genotype, phenotype, allele, homozygous/heterozygous, dominant/recessive, polygenic, heritability).
- Practice answer 1: 500–750 word critique of Herrnstein & Murray using lecture evidence (include Flynn effect, Lewontin, twin/adoption limitations).
- Practice answer 2: Explain PKU as a test-case for gene × environment interaction in 250 words with Herzberg & Diamond 1993 conclusion.
- Diagram practice: Draw and label the heritability variance decomposition figure from the slide; annotate what H^2 = G/V means practically.
- Memorise the three classic designs to estimate heritability and one strength and one limitation for each.

### Source 4: Russell Lecture10 2026.pdf — Psych109 (Lecture 10: Evolutionary Psychology — animal minds)  
Source identity (explicit): Source number 4; file name / display name “Russell Lecture10 2026.pdf”; slide title shown on cover “Lecture 10: Evolutionary Psychology - animal minds”; course Psych 109; Lecturer: Russell Gray. #### Opening frame / central question
What does comparative psychology tell us about which cognitive abilities are uniquely human and which are shared with other animals — and how should we test those claims experimentally?  
The lecture asks, repeatedly and concretely: Are tool manufacture, language, and statistical (probabilistic) reasoning uniquely human? If not, how do we distinguish shared ancestry (homology), convergent solutions, and simple associative explanations from genuine cognitive similarity?

#### Source structure / lecture flow
The lecture is organised as a sequence of themes/slides. I follow the slide order and report slide titles or visible text where present.

1. Cover / frame (page 1)
   - Slide title: “Lecture 10: Evolutionary Psychology - animal minds” (Psych 109, Lecturer: Russell Gray). Cover image juxtaposes a human and a non-human primate (visual rhetoric: human–animal continuity and tension).

2. Learning objectives (page 3)
   - By the end you should be able to:
     - Explain what comparative psychologists do.
     - Understand why controlled experiments are required to discriminate alternative accounts of animal cognition.
     - Understand the difference between homology and convergence.
     - Know the four things natural selection requires.
     - Think through the broader implications of animal cognition findings.

3. Lecture plan (appears repeatedly across slides)
   - 1) What is comparative psychology
   - 2) Does tool manufacture make us unique?
   - 3) Key evolutionary concepts
   - 4) Does language make us unique?
   - 5) Are we the only creatures who can do stats?
   - 6) Broader implications

4. Why study animal minds? (slide content)
   - Short rationale: we are related to every living thing that has ever lived — comparative study reveals shared and unique components.

5. Two broad interpretive camps (slide: “Romantics vs Killjoys”)
   - Romantics: attribute complex capacities (tool manufacture, causal reasoning, theory of mind, episodic-like memory, empathy, cooperation) to animals.
   - Killjoys: argue simple learning principles explain behaviour; urge caution.

6. Homology vs convergence (slides)
   - Homology defined as “shared inherited structures” (example given: vertebrate limb, frontal lobe).
   - Convergent evolution defined: independent evolution of a similar outcome where selection favoured the same solution (diagram with Ancestor1 & Ancestor2 → Same outcome).

7. Natural selection basics (slide)
   - Four requirements for natural selection: 1) Variation 2) Heritable (mutation) 3) Design differences (i.e. differential fit) 4) Competition.

8. Tool manufacture question (slides & examples)
   - Louis Leakey quote “Man the tool maker” placed as historic context.
   - Tool manufacture in other primates (image: chimp tools).
   - New Caledonian crow tool culture and pandanus-leaf tools referenced (Hunt & Gray 2003, 2004).

9. Language question (sequence of slides & case studies)

     - Nim Chimpsky (Terrace et al.): “drill” training, no grammar — example sentences recorded.

#### Key concepts and definitions
(Concept — definition — where it appears — why it matters)

- Comparative psychology — study of cognitive abilities across species to infer shared/unique mechanisms (Learning objectives slide / lecture plan). Matters because it frames methods and inference rules used to compare minds.

- Homology — shared inherited structure or trait from common ancestor (slide “Homology – shared inherited structures e.g the vertebrate limb, frontal lobe”). Important to decide whether a trait is shared because of ancestry.

- Convergent evolution — independent evolution of similar traits where natural selection favoured the same outcome (slide with Ancestor1/Ancestor2 diagram). Important because similar cognitive outcomes can arise without shared ancestry.

- Natural selection (4 requirements) — Variation, Heritable (mutation), Design differences (differential fitness), Competition (slide “Natural selection requires four things”). Matters because evolution-of-cognition claims must meet these criteria.

- Tool manufacture / tool culture — behavioural techniques to create or use tools (slides on New Caledonian crows, Leakey quote). Relevant to claims about uniquely human technology and cultural transmission.

- Language (in context) — symbolic, syntactic, generative communication (slides on Gua, Viki, Nim, Kanzi). Key to distinguishing human-specific capacities.

- Probabilistic reasoning — predicting likelihoods based on sampled evidence (slides on kea token experiments). Central for claims about animals doing “statistics” or reasoning under uncertainty.

- Associative / learning explanations — explanation using simple reinforcement learning or conditioning (present implicitly in “Killjoys” framing). Crucial alternative hypothesis to sophisticated cognitive explanations.

#### Important studies / examples / cases (structured entries)
I extract the studies/examples explicitly present in the slides and present them in a teaching-friendly format.

1) New Caledonian crow tool culture (Hunt & Gray 2003, 2004)
- Question:
  - Do New Caledonian crows manufacture tools and cultural traditions of tool use exist outside humans, and what does that imply about uniqueness of human tool manufacture?
- Method / process:
  - Field and observational studies documented manufacture and use of pandanus-leaf tools by New Caledonian crows; reported patterned variation and transmission suggesting cultural traditions (Hunt & Gray 2003 Proc R Soc B; 2004 Proc R Soc B).
- Result / answer:
  - Crows manufacture specialized pandanus-leaf tools and show localised variation consistent with cultural transmission.
- Meaning:
  - Tool manufacture is not uniquely human; independent, ecologically-driven evolution of manufacture can produce sophisticated tool behaviour (convergent evolution + cultural transmission in a bird lineage).
- Limitation(s):
  - Complexity and functions of tools differ from human technology; ecological context matters; observational methods less able to prove cognitive mechanisms (planning, causal models) without controlled experiments.
- Exam use:
  - Use as an example of convergent evolution and cultural transmission in non-primate taxa; discuss how this complicates claims of human uniqueness in “tool culture.” High-scoring answers link to homology/convergence and experimental controls needed to distinguish learning rules from planning.

2) Early ape language projects: Gua and Viki (slide)
- Question:
  - Can apes acquire human language when raised with humans?
- Method / process:
  - Cross-rearing (Gua) and intensive training (Viki).
- Result / answer:
  - Limited success: Viki produced ~3–4 words (slide lists “Mama, papa, up, cup”), Gua similarly limited.
- Meaning:
  - Mere exposure or rearing is insufficient for ape mastery of spoken human language; vocal anatomy and cognitive-linguistic limitations are implicated.
- Limitations:
  - Many early studies conflated production limits (vocal tract) with symbolic/grammatical competence.
- Exam use:
  - Short case to show methodological issues in early language claims; contrast with later lexigram studies.

3) Nim Chimpsky (Terrace et al.; slides quoting example utterances)
- Question:
  - Can chimpanzees learn grammar (syntax) like humans using intensive training?
- Method / process:
  - Intensive training (“drill”) teaching sign-language-like tokens; recording utterances.
- Result / answer:
  - Chimp utterances were often repetitive, lacked compositional grammar; examples listed (“Nim eat Nim eat”, “Me gum me gum”).
- Meaning:
  - Learning isolated symbols occurs, but spontaneous grammatical composition observed in humans is not clearly replicated; Terrace et al. argued against ape grammar mastery.
- Limitations:
  - Training methods and interpretation of communication goals; criticism that experimental set-up discouraged natural conversational structure.
- Exam use:
  - Classic counter-evidence against ape language parity; use quotations to illustrate qualitative differences between symbol use and syntax.

4) Kanzi (bonobo) — lexigrams & speech synthesiser (slide)
- Question:
  - Can apes understand symbolic referents and use them flexibly?
- Method / process:
  - Lexigram keyboard training, exposure in more naturalistic settings, tests for comprehension and production.
- Result / answer:
  - Kanzi demonstrated comprehension of many lexigrams (~100 words cited), could use symbols to request items, showed genuine symbolic learning but not conversational, generative grammar use.
- Meaning:
  - Apes can achieve impressive symbolic competence but fall short of human-like syntax/semantic organisation. The slide summarises: “Truly symbolic — 100 words — Reactive, not grasp functionality of language.”
- Limitations:
  - Differences in spontaneous generative syntax remain; comprehension vs productive syntax distinction.
- Exam use:
  - Use to show the strongest evidence for non-human symbolic processing; contrast Kanzi’s achievements with Nim’s failure to show syntax.

5) Kea probabilistic reasoning experiment (slides & images; photographer Amalia Bastos)
- Question:
  - Can kea (a parrot species) use probabilistic reasoning to infer which unseen sample is more likely to yield a reward?
- Method / process:
  - Training phase: kea learn token values — black token exchangeable for food (reward), orange token not rewarded.
  - Test Condition 1 (described on slides):
    - Experimenter places left hand in Jar A (100 black, 20 orange) and right hand in Jar B (20 black, 100 orange). Experimenter draws a token from each jar (hidden in closed fist behind a cardboard barrier) and offers both closed fists; kea must choose a hand to receive the token.
    - Kea cannot see the specific token drawn (only know jar composition and sampling procedure).
- Result / answer (slide implies experiment focuses on this test; the slide set-up is shown but numeric outcome is not printed in the screenshot excerpts):
  - Slide intent: this experimental design tests whether kea prefer the hand sampled from the jar with higher proportion of rewarding tokens (i.e., show probability-based choice) rather than just associative preference.
- Meaning:
  - If kea systematically choose the hand sampled from the higher-probability jar, this is evidence they integrate prior frequencies into decision-making — a form of probabilistic reasoning/statistical inference — not forced associative learning from visible token identity.
- Limitations / critical controls:
  - Need to rule out simple cues (smell, subtle experimenter cues), sampling bias, or choice based on absolute counts rather than probability. The lecture stresses the importance of experiments to discriminate alternative accounts (slide: Learning objectives).
- Exam use:
  - Rich example for methodology: show how to design tests that distinguish associative learning from probabilistic inference. High-scoring answers explain training, counter-balancing, and alternative explanations and how to rule them out.

#### Common misunderstandings (source-specific) and corrections
1. Misunderstanding: If an animal chooses from a jar with more “good” tokens, it proves human-like probabilistic reasoning.  
   Correction: A preference for the high-proportion jar is necessary but not sufficient evidence. Alternative explanations (olfactory cues, experimenter cueing, local enhancement, side bias, simple associative learning based on past reinforcement rates) must be ruled out with controls, counterbalancing, and transfer tests.

2. Misunderstanding: New Caledonian crows having tools means they have human-like tool culture.  
   Correction: The presence of tool manufacture and localized traditions shows convergence and cultural transmission, but not equivalence to the cumulative, open-ended technology that characterises humans. Ecological explanations and independent evolution must be considered.

#### Key concepts and definitions
Below is a compact concept table that uses the wording and slide locations in the source. Each entry: definition (as the slide implies), simple explanation, where it appears in the slides, and why it matters.

- EEG (Electroencephalography)  
  - Definition: Measurement of differences in electrical activity across the skull to infer underlying cortical activity.  
  - Simple explanation: Scalp electrodes record voltage changes over time produced by synchronous postsynaptic potentials in cortical neurons.  
  - Where in source: Slides 7–16, 18–23.  
  - Why it matters: Provides direct recording of neural electrical activity with very high temporal precision — useful for questions about timing of cognitive processes.

- MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging)  
  - Definition: A structural imaging technique that uses a strong magnetic field and radiofrequency pulses to generate images of tissue based on proton properties.  
  - Simple explanation: Protons in tissues align with the scanner’s field; RF pulses perturb them; energy released on relaxation is recorded and mapped to form images.  
  - Where: Slides 24–37.  
  - Why it matters: Gives high spatial resolution images of brain anatomy and is the primary method for structural brain investigation and clinical detection of abnormalities.

- fMRI (Functional MRI) / BOLD (Blood Oxygen Level Dependent) signal  
  - Definition: A technique that infers neural activity from localized changes in blood oxygenation and flow; the BOLD signal arises because oxygenated and deoxygenated haemoglobin have different magnetic properties.  
  - Simple explanation: Active neurons recruit more oxygenated blood than nearby tissue, changing local magnetic signal captured by MRI sequences.  
  - Where: Slides 38–44.  
  - Why it matters: Allows spatially detailed mapping of brain regions that co‑activate with tasks, but is an indirect and temporally slow measure of neural activity.

- Temporal resolution  
  - Definition: How precisely a method can resolve events in time (milliseconds vs seconds).  
  - Where: Slide 11–12 (EEG sampling), Slide 44 (fMRI temporal limitation).  
  - Why: Determines whether a technique can answer "when" questions about brain processes.

- Spatial resolution  
  - Definition: How precisely a method can localize signal to a particular location/voxel in the brain.  
  - Where: Slides 23 (EEG weakness), 26 & 44 (MRI/fMRI strength).  
  - Why: Determines whether a technique can answer "where" questions.

- Signal vs Noise (measurement model)  
  - Definition: Measured data = true signal (activity related to process) + noise (everything else).  
  - Where: Slide 17.  
  - Why: Central to data preprocessing, artifact rejection, and experimental design for neuroimaging.

- Voxel / Pixel (spatial sampling units)  
  - Definition: 3D unit of MR image volume (voxel); higher magnetic field and smaller voxels → higher spatial resolution.  
  - Where: Slide 26.  
  - Why: Determines image resolution and ability to detect small structures.

- Sampling rate (e.g., 1000 Hz)  
  - Definition: How frequently measurements are taken (samples per second).  
  - Where: Slide 12.  
  - Why: Key to temporal resolution for EEG.

#### What this source uniquely contributes

- Clear, exam‑relevant statements of strengths and weaknesses: EEG = excellent temporal resolution but poor spatial resolution; MRI/fMRI = excellent spatial resolution but poor temporal resolution. This is the slide set’s central comparative contribution.  
- A concise, accurate BOLD explanation: links neuronal activation → blood flow & oxygenation → magnetic signal differences, enabling students to explain why fMRI is indirect and temporally sluggish.
- Practical considerations: mentions cost, proximity to scanner, noise sources, and gel/contact issues in EEG — useful for method sections in essays.
